name: Economics Advanced
description: A rigorous exploration of microeconomic and macroeconomic theory, including market structures, game theory, fiscal and monetary policy, international trade, and development economics.

modules.title: Advanced Demand Theory
modules.objectives: Analyze consumer preferences using indifference curves and budget constraints; derive demand curves from utility maximization; distinguish income and substitution effects.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 6 — Consumption Choices
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Demand Curve as Marginal Benefit
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Indifference Curves and Budget Constraints Explained
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Demand Curve Practice Exercises
modules.assignment: Draw a consumer's indifference map and budget constraint, then show graphically how a price decrease in Good X creates both a substitution effect and an income effect for a normal good.

modules.title: Advanced Supply & Production Theory
modules.objectives: Apply production functions to analyze short-run and long-run costs; distinguish diminishing marginal returns from economies of scale; derive a firm's supply curve from marginal cost.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 7 — Production, Costs, and Industry Structure
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Short-Run vs Long-Run Costs of Production
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Production and Costs Practice
modules.assignment: Given a table of total product values, calculate marginal product and average product; graph the total cost, marginal cost, average total cost, and average variable cost curves.

modules.title: Market Structures: Perfect Competition
modules.objectives: Define the characteristics of perfectly competitive markets; determine the profit-maximizing output using MR = MC; analyze short-run and long-run equilibrium outcomes.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 8 — Perfect Competition
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Perfect Competition: Short Run and Long Run
modules.assignment: Using a given cost schedule, determine the profit-maximizing quantity and economic profit or loss for a perfectly competitive firm at three different market prices; explain the long-run adjustment process.

modules.title: Monopoly & Monopolistic Competition
modules.objectives: Explain how a monopolist maximizes profit by setting MR = MC and charging above marginal cost; analyze monopolistic competition in the short and long run; evaluate welfare losses from market power.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 9 — Monopoly and Ch 10 — Monopolistic Competition
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Monopoly vs Monopolistic Competition Explained
modules.assignment: Given a monopolist's demand schedule and cost data, find the profit-maximizing price and quantity, calculate deadweight loss, and compare the outcome to a perfectly competitive benchmark.

modules.title: Oligopoly & Game Theory
modules.objectives: Describe the characteristics of oligopoly; apply game theory concepts including the prisoner's dilemma and Nash equilibrium to business strategy; analyze cartel behavior and anti-trust policy.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 10.2 — Oligopoly
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Oligopolies and Game Theory
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Nash Equilibrium and Prisoner's Dilemma
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Oligopoly and Game Theory Practice
modules.assignment: Construct a payoff matrix for a two-firm pricing game, identify the Nash equilibrium, and explain why the equilibrium may not be the socially optimal outcome; relate to real-world antitrust cases.

modules.title: Factor Markets: Labor & Capital
modules.titleZh: 要素市场：劳动力与资本
modules.objectives: Analyze labor demand as a derived demand using marginal revenue product; explain wage determination in competitive and monopsonistic labor markets; discuss the role of capital markets in investment decisions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 14 — Labor Markets and Income
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Marginal Revenue Product and Wage Determination
modules.assignment: Using a marginal revenue product table, determine how many workers a profit-maximizing firm will hire at various wage rates; graphically illustrate the effect of a minimum wage on employment in a competitive labor market.

modules.title: Market Failures & Externalities
modules.objectives: Identify sources of market failure; analyze negative and positive externalities using supply-demand diagrams; evaluate government interventions including taxes, subsidies, regulations, and cap-and-trade systems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 12 — Environmental Protection and Negative Externalities
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Market Failures, Externalities, and Government Solutions
modules.assignment: For both a negative production externality (e.g., pollution) and a positive consumption externality (e.g., education), draw the relevant supply and demand diagrams showing the market failure and illustrate the corrective policy.

modules.title: Public Goods & Information Economics
modules.titleZh: 公共物品与信息经济学
modules.objectives: Distinguish public goods from private goods using non-excludability and non-rivalry; explain the free-rider problem; analyze how asymmetric information creates adverse selection and moral hazard.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 13 — Public Goods and Common Resources
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Asymmetric Information, Adverse Selection, and Moral Hazard
modules.assignment: Classify ten goods/services as private goods, public goods, club goods, or common resources; write a paragraph explaining how adverse selection in health insurance markets can lead to market failure.

modules.title: Macroeconomic Measurement
modules.objectives: Calculate GDP using expenditure and income approaches; distinguish real from nominal GDP; interpret GDP deflator and CPI; analyze limitations of GDP as a measure of welfare.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 19 — Measuring the Size of the Economy: GDP
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — GDP and the Circular Flow of Income
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Real vs Nominal GDP and the GDP Deflator
modules.assignment: Using provided national income data, calculate nominal GDP, real GDP (using a base year), and the GDP deflator; write a critical analysis of why GDP may not fully capture a nation's standard of living.

modules.title: Business Cycles & Growth Theory
modules.objectives: Identify the phases of the business cycle and leading/lagging economic indicators; explain the Solow growth model and its predictions regarding capital accumulation and technological progress; discuss causes of long-run economic growth.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 25 — Economic Growth
modules.assignment: Research one historical US business cycle recession; identify its causes, key indicators, peak and trough dates, and discuss which government policies were used in response.

modules.title: Fiscal Policy
modules.objectives: Explain how government spending and taxation affect aggregate demand; analyze expansionary and contractionary fiscal policy using the AD-AS model; evaluate the multiplier effect, crowding out, and automatic stabilizers.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 30 — Government Budgets and Fiscal Policy
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Fiscal Policy and Aggregate Demand
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Fiscal Policy: Expansionary vs Contractionary
modules.assignment: Draw an AD-AS diagram showing a recessionary gap, then illustrate how discretionary fiscal policy closes the gap; calculate the spending multiplier given a MPC of 0.8 and explain crowding out.

modules.title: Monetary Policy & Central Banking
modules.objectives: Describe the Federal Reserve's structure and tools; explain how open market operations affect the money supply and interest rates; analyze the transmission mechanism from monetary policy to real GDP and inflation.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 28 — The Money Supply and the Federal Reserve
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The Federal Reserve and Monetary Policy
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How the Fed Controls Interest Rates
modules.assignment: Trace through the complete monetary policy transmission mechanism: starting from the Fed buying $10 billion in government bonds, describe each step until the final effect on inflation and real GDP.

modules.title: International Trade Theory
modules.objectives: Apply comparative advantage to explain gains from trade; analyze the effects of tariffs and quotas on domestic consumers, producers, and government revenue; evaluate the arguments for and against free trade.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Principles of Economics 2e Ch 33 — International Trade
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Comparative Advantage and Gains from Trade
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Tariffs and Quotas: Effects and Analysis
modules.assignment: Given production possibilities data for two countries, calculate opportunity costs, identify comparative advantages, and determine the range of mutually beneficial trading prices; draw the world price tariff diagram showing welfare effects.

quizQuestions.question: When the price of a good rises and a consumer buys less of it because it is relatively more expensive than substitutes, this is called the:
quizQuestions.answer: Substitution effect
quizQuestions.explanation: The substitution effect refers to the change in quantity demanded caused by a change in the relative price of a good, holding real income constant.

quizQuestions.question: On an indifference curve diagram, the slope of the budget constraint represents:
options: The marginal utility of Good X
The consumer's marginal rate of substitution
quizQuestions.answer: The ratio of prices (Px/Py)
quizQuestions.explanation: The budget constraint's slope equals the negative price ratio (−Px/Py), reflecting the market trade-off between the two goods.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "When the price of a good rises and a consumer buys less of it because it is relatively more expensive than substitutes, this is called the:" is: Substitution effect.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Substitution effect is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The law of diminishing marginal returns states that as more units of a variable input are added to fixed inputs:
quizQuestions.answer: Marginal product eventually decreases
quizQuestions.explanation: Diminishing marginal returns occur in the short run when each additional unit of the variable input adds less to total output than the previous unit.

quizQuestions.question: In the short run, which of the following costs does NOT change with output?
quizQuestions.answer: Fixed costs
quizQuestions.explanation: Fixed costs, such as rent or equipment leases, remain constant in the short run regardless of the level of output produced.

quizQuestions.question: The marginal cost curve intersects the average total cost curve at:
options: The point where ATC equals AFC
quizQuestions.answer: The minimum point of ATC
quizQuestions.explanation: When MC is below ATC, ATC is falling; when MC is above ATC, ATC is rising. Therefore, MC crosses ATC at its minimum point.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The law of diminishing marginal returns states that as more units of a variable input are added to fixed inputs:" is: Marginal product eventually decreases.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Marginal product eventually decreases is correct.

quizQuestions.question: In a perfectly competitive market, an individual firm is a price-taker because:
options: It is one of many sellers of an identical product
quizQuestions.answer: It is one of many sellers of an identical product
quizQuestions.explanation: With many identical sellers, no single firm can influence the market price; each must accept the equilibrium price as given.

quizQuestions.question: For a perfectly competitive firm, price equals:
options: Marginal cost only at the profit-maximizing output
Average revenue and marginal revenue at all output levels
Marginal revenue only when profits are zero
Average total cost at all output levels
quizQuestions.answer: Average revenue and marginal revenue at all output levels
quizQuestions.explanation: Because a competitive firm is a price-taker, P = AR = MR at every level of output.

quizQuestions.question: A perfectly competitive firm should shut down in the short run if:
options: Price is less than average total cost
Price is less than average variable cost
quizQuestions.answer: Price is less than average variable cost
quizQuestions.explanation: A firm shuts down when it cannot cover variable costs — if P < AVC, losses from producing exceed losses from shutting down (which equal fixed costs).

quizQuestions.question: A monopolist maximizes profit by producing where:
options: Average revenue equals average total cost
quizQuestions.answer: Marginal revenue equals marginal cost
quizQuestions.explanation: Like all profit-maximizing firms, a monopolist produces where MR = MC, but unlike competitive firms, it then charges a price above MC as determined by the demand curve.

quizQuestions.question: For a monopolist, marginal revenue is less than price because:
options: Selling one more unit requires lowering price on all units sold
The demand curve is perfectly elastic
quizQuestions.answer: Selling one more unit requires lowering price on all units sold
quizQuestions.explanation: A monopolist faces a downward-sloping demand curve; to sell an additional unit it must lower price for all units, making MR < P.

quizQuestions.question: Deadweight loss under monopoly arises because the monopolist:
options: Produces too much at too low a price
Produces too little at too high a price
Earns zero economic profit in the long run
quizQuestions.answer: Produces too little at too high a price
quizQuestions.explanation: The monopolist restricts output below the competitive level and charges a price above MC, creating a deadweight loss — transactions that would benefit both buyer and seller do not occur.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a key characteristic of monopolistic competition?
options: A single seller with high barriers to entry
Strategic interdependence among a few firms
quizQuestions.answer: Differentiated products and many sellers
quizQuestions.explanation: Monopolistic competition involves many sellers each selling differentiated products, giving each firm some pricing power while still facing competition.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes an oligopoly?
options: A market with many small firms selling identical products
A market dominated by a few interdependent firms
A single seller with no close substitutes
A market with free entry and differentiated products
quizQuestions.answer: A market dominated by a few interdependent firms
quizQuestions.explanation: Oligopoly is characterized by a small number of firms whose pricing and output decisions are mutually interdependent.

quizQuestions.question: In a prisoner's dilemma game, the Nash equilibrium is often a poor outcome because:
options: Both players cooperate and maximize joint profits
Each player's dominant strategy leads to a worse collective outcome
One player always defects while the other cooperates
Government intervention always prevents the game from being played
quizQuestions.answer: Each player's dominant strategy leads to a worse collective outcome
quizQuestions.explanation: In the prisoner's dilemma, individual rationality (choosing the dominant strategy) leads to a collectively suboptimal equilibrium.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following best describes an oligopoly" is: A market dominated by a few interdependent firms.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. A market dominated by a few interdependent firms is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "In a prisoner's dilemma game, the Nash equilibrium is often a poor outcome because:" is: Both players cooperate and maximize joint profits.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Each player's dominant strategy leads to a worse collective outcome.

quizQuestions.question: A firm's demand for labor is derived from:
options: The demand for the good or service the labor produces
The number of workers in the labor force
quizQuestions.answer: The demand for the good or service the labor produces
quizQuestions.explanation: Labor demand is derived demand — firms hire workers not for labor itself but to produce goods and services consumers want.

quizQuestions.question: Marginal revenue product of labor (MRPL) is calculated as:
options: Marginal product of labor × price of output
Change in total cost ÷ change in labor
quizQuestions.answer: Marginal product of labor × price of output
quizQuestions.explanation: MRPL = MPL × P (in a competitive output market), representing the additional revenue generated by hiring one more worker.

quizQuestions.question: A profit-maximizing firm hires workers until:
options: Average revenue product equals the wage rate
The last worker hired earns zero marginal product
quizQuestions.answer: MRPL equals the wage rate
quizQuestions.explanation: Firms maximize profit in labor markets by hiring up to the point where MRPL = W, the same MR = MC logic applied to factor markets.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A firm's demand for labor is derived from:" is: The demand for the good or service the labor produces.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. The demand for the good or service the labor produces is correct.

quizQuestions.question: A negative externality occurs when a market transaction:
options: Creates benefits for third parties not involved in the transaction
Imposes costs on third parties not involved in the transaction
Results in a price below equilibrium
Causes the supply curve to shift right
quizQuestions.answer: Imposes costs on third parties not involved in the transaction
quizQuestions.explanation: A negative externality (e.g., pollution) means the social cost of production exceeds the private cost, causing overproduction from a social welfare perspective.

quizQuestions.question: When a market has a negative externality, the socially optimal quantity is:
options: Greater than the market equilibrium quantity
Equal to the market equilibrium quantity
Less than the market equilibrium quantity
quizQuestions.answer: Less than the market equilibrium quantity
quizQuestions.explanation: With a negative externality, social marginal cost exceeds private marginal cost, so the free market overproduces. The socially optimal quantity is lower.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A negative externality occurs when a market transaction:" is: Imposes costs on third parties not involved in the transaction.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Imposes costs on third parties not involved in the transaction is correct.

quizQuestions.question: A public good is characterized by:
options: Being provided exclusively by private firms
quizQuestions.answer: Non-excludability and non-rivalry
quizQuestions.explanation: Public goods are non-excludable (you cannot prevent non-payers from consuming them) and non-rival (one person's use does not reduce availability to others).

quizQuestions.question: The free-rider problem arises because:
options: Public goods are too expensive to produce
People can consume a public good without paying for it
Private firms always over-provide public goods
quizQuestions.answer: People can consume a public good without paying for it
quizQuestions.explanation: Since public goods are non-excludable, individuals can benefit without paying, leading private markets to underprovide them.

quizQuestions.question: Adverse selection in insurance markets occurs when:
options: Insurers can perfectly observe policyholders' risk levels
High-risk individuals are more likely to purchase insurance than low-risk individuals
Insurance companies collude to raise premiums
quizQuestions.answer: High-risk individuals are more likely to purchase insurance than low-risk individuals
quizQuestions.explanation: Adverse selection results from asymmetric information: high-risk individuals seek insurance more than low-risk individuals, leading insurers to face a riskier-than-average pool.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A public good is characterized by:" is: Non-excludability and non-rivalry.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Non-excludability and non-rivalry is correct.

quizQuestions.question: GDP measures the:
options: Total income earned by a country's citizens abroad and domestically
Total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given period
Total value of all goods produced, including intermediate goods
Total government spending within a country
quizQuestions.answer: Total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given period
quizQuestions.explanation: GDP is the monetary value of all final goods and services produced within a nation's borders during a specific time period, typically one year.

quizQuestions.question: In the expenditure approach to GDP, the four components are:
options: Consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports
quizQuestions.answer: Consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports
quizQuestions.explanation: GDP = C + I + G + (X - M), where C is consumption, I is investment, G is government spending, and (X - M) is net exports.

quizQuestions.question: The GDP deflator is used to:
options: Convert nominal GDP into real GDP by adjusting for price level changes
quizQuestions.answer: Convert nominal GDP into real GDP by adjusting for price level changes
quizQuestions.explanation: The GDP deflator = (Nominal GDP / Real GDP) × 100, allowing economists to separate changes in output from changes in prices.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: Complete: In the context of the topic, ___ refers to "if nominal gdp grows by 5% and the price level rises by 3%, then real gdp grew by approximately:."
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: 2%.

quizQuestions.question: The four phases of the business cycle in order are:
quizQuestions.answer: Expansion, peak, contraction, trough
quizQuestions.explanation: The business cycle moves from expansion (growth) to peak (maximum output) to contraction/recession (declining output) to trough (minimum output) and then back to expansion.

quizQuestions.question: According to the Solow growth model, long-run per capita growth is driven by:
quizQuestions.answer: Technological progress
quizQuestions.explanation: In the Solow model, capital accumulation and labor force growth have only level effects; only technological progress generates sustained long-run per capita economic growth.

quizQuestions.question: A leading economic indicator is one that:
options: Moves in the same direction as the economy at the same time
Changes after the economy has already changed
Changes before the economy as a whole changes
quizQuestions.answer: Changes before the economy as a whole changes
quizQuestions.explanation: Leading indicators — like stock prices, building permits, and new orders — tend to predict future economic activity and change before the economy turns.

quizQuestions.question: According to the Solow model, poorer countries tend to grow faster than richer countries because of:
quizQuestions.answer: Diminishing returns to capital
quizQuestions.explanation: Diminishing returns to capital mean each additional unit of capital adds less output. Poor countries with less capital per worker gain more from each new unit — predicting convergence.

quizQuestions.question: Expansionary fiscal policy involves:
options: Raising taxes and cutting government spending
Cutting taxes and increasing government spending
quizQuestions.answer: Cutting taxes and increasing government spending
quizQuestions.explanation: Expansionary fiscal policy increases aggregate demand to stimulate the economy during a recession by reducing taxes (boosting disposable income) and/or increasing government spending.

quizQuestions.question: If the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.75, the spending multiplier equals:
quizQuestions.explanation: Spending multiplier = 1 / (1 - MPC) = 1 / (1 - 0.75) = 1 / 0.25 = 4. An initial increase in spending cycles through the economy four times.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Expansionary fiscal policy involves:" is: Cutting taxes and increasing government spending.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Cutting taxes and increasing government spending is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "If the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.75, the spending multiplier equals:" is: 1.75.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: 4.

quizQuestions.question: When the Federal Reserve buys government securities in the open market, it:
options: Decreases the money supply and raises interest rates
Increases the money supply and lowers interest rates
Has no effect on bank reserves
quizQuestions.answer: Increases the money supply and lowers interest rates
quizQuestions.explanation: Open market purchases inject reserves into the banking system, expanding the money supply and lowering interest rates — an expansionary monetary policy action.

quizQuestions.question: The discount rate is the interest rate that:
options: The Federal Reserve charges banks for short-term loans
The federal government pays on Treasury bonds
Consumers pay on credit card balances
quizQuestions.answer: The Federal Reserve charges banks for short-term loans
quizQuestions.explanation: The discount rate is the rate the Fed charges depository institutions for borrowing reserves directly from the Federal Reserve's discount window.

quizQuestions.question: If the Fed raises the federal funds rate, the expected result is:
options: Decreased borrowing, lower investment, and reduced inflationary pressure
A larger money supply and lower unemployment
quizQuestions.answer: Decreased borrowing, lower investment, and reduced inflationary pressure
quizQuestions.explanation: Higher interest rates make borrowing more expensive, reducing consumption and investment, slowing aggregate demand and cooling inflation.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "When the Federal Reserve buys government securities in the open market, it:" is: Increases the money supply and lowers interest rates.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Increases the money supply and lowers interest rates is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Comparative advantage is based on:
options: Relative opportunity costs of production between countries
The total amount of resources a country possesses
The exchange rate between two currencies
quizQuestions.answer: Relative opportunity costs of production between countries
quizQuestions.explanation: A country has a comparative advantage in producing a good if it can do so at a lower opportunity cost than another country, regardless of absolute productivity.

quizQuestions.question: The gains from trade based on comparative advantage allow countries to:
options: Consume beyond their individual production possibilities
quizQuestions.answer: Consume beyond their individual production possibilities
quizQuestions.explanation: By specializing in goods where they have comparative advantage and trading, countries can consume combinations of goods outside their own production possibility frontier.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Comparative advantage is based on:" is: Relative opportunity costs of production between countries.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Relative opportunity costs of production between countries is correct.

questions.question: On an indifference curve diagram, a point inside the budget constraint indicates:
options: The consumer is spending more than their income
The consumer is not spending all available income
questions.answer: The consumer is not spending all available income
questions.explanation: A point inside (below) the budget constraint means the consumer is not fully utilizing their income, so they could reach a higher indifference curve.

questions.question: When a firm experiences diseconomies of scale, its long-run average total cost:
questions.explanation: Diseconomies of scale occur when expansion leads to coordination problems, bureaucracy, or other inefficiencies that raise average costs.

questions.question: For a perfectly competitive firm in short-run equilibrium producing at a loss, the firm should continue producing as long as:
options: Marginal revenue equals average total cost
questions.explanation: As long as revenue covers variable costs (P > AVC), the firm minimizes losses by producing rather than shutting down, because fixed costs must still be paid.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Price discrimination by a monopolist involves:" is: Charging different prices to different consumers based on willingness to pay.
questions.explanation: True. Charging different prices to different consumers based on willingness to pay is correct.

questions.question: In a duopoly Cournot game, each firm chooses its output assuming:
options: The other firm will match any price change
The other firm's output remains constant
The firms will collude to maximize joint profits
The government will regulate the market
questions.answer: The other firm's output remains constant
questions.explanation: In the Cournot model, each firm maximizes profit by choosing output while treating the other firm's output as fixed, leading to a Nash equilibrium.

questions.question: If an increase in the wage rate shifts the labor supply curve to the left, this is most consistent with:
questions.explanation: At higher wages, workers may work fewer hours (the income effect dominates the substitution effect), creating a backward-bending supply curve.

questions.question: The tragedy of the commons refers to:
options: The tendency for governments to underprovide public goods
The overexploitation of common resources due to lack of property rights
Market failure caused by asymmetric information
questions.answer: The overexploitation of common resources due to lack of property rights
questions.explanation: Without ownership of shared resources (fisheries, grazing land), individuals have incentives to overuse them, depleting them below the socially optimal level.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A subsidy given to firms with positive externalities is intended to:" is: Decrease production and raise prices.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Increase production toward the socially optimal level.

questions.question: Signaling in labor markets (e.g., college education) is used to address which information problem?
questions.explanation: Signaling — sending observable, credible signals of unobservable quality — helps employers distinguish high-productivity from low-productivity workers, mitigating adverse selection.

questions.question: Compared to perfect competition, monopolistic competition results in:
options: Productive efficiency with no excess capacity
Zero economic profit achieved at minimum ATC
questions.explanation: In long-run monopolistic competition equilibrium, firms produce on the downward-sloping part of ATC (not minimum ATC), resulting in excess capacity but offering consumers product variety.

questions.question: Which market structure is most likely to engage in non-price competition (advertising, product differentiation)?
questions.explanation: Firms in monopolistic competition and oligopoly sell differentiated products and compete heavily on advertising, quality, and branding in addition to price.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "An increase in consumer income causes the demand for an inferior good to:" is: Decrease.
questions.explanation: True. Decrease is correct.

questions.question: The minimum point of the average variable cost (AVC) curve is known as the:
questions.explanation: The minimum AVC is the shutdown point — if market price falls below this level, the firm minimizes losses by producing nothing in the short run.

questions.question: Game theory's concept of a dominant strategy means that:
options: A player always wins the game
A strategy is best regardless of what the other player does
questions.answer: A strategy is best regardless of what the other player does
questions.explanation: A dominant strategy yields the highest payoff for a player no matter what strategies the other players choose.

questions.question: Consumer surplus is the area:
options: Below the supply curve and above the price
Below the demand curve and above the equilibrium price
Between the supply and demand curves at any quantity
Equal to total revenue minus total cost
questions.answer: Below the demand curve and above the equilibrium price
questions.explanation: Consumer surplus represents the benefit consumers receive beyond what they pay — it is the area below the demand curve and above the market price.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A price ceiling set below the equilibrium price will cause:" is: A surplus.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: A shortage.

questions.question: The income effect of a wage increase predicts that workers will:
options: Work fewer hours because they can afford more leisure
Supply more labor as wages rise indefinitely
questions.answer: Work fewer hours because they can afford more leisure
questions.explanation: The income effect of a wage rise means workers can achieve their target income with fewer hours, potentially reducing labor supply — contributing to the backward-bending supply curve.

questions.question: Nominal GDP in Year 1 is $10 trillion and in Year 2 is $11 trillion. If the price level rose by 5%, real GDP growth was approximately:
During a recession, automatic stabilizers cause the federal budget to:
questions.explanation: During recessions, unemployment insurance payouts rise and tax revenues fall, automatically increasing the budget deficit without new legislation.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "In the AD-AS model, a supply shock that increases production costs would:" is: Shift aggregate supply left, raising prices and lowering output.
questions.explanation: True. Shift aggregate supply left, raising prices and lowering output is correct.

questions.question: If the reserve requirement is 20%, the money multiplier is:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 5.

questions.question: An expansionary inflationary gap in the AD-AS model would be best addressed by:
options: The Fed raising interest rates or Congress cutting spending
Buying government bonds in the open market
questions.answer: The Fed raising interest rates or Congress cutting spending
questions.explanation: An inflationary gap (actual GDP > potential) requires contractionary policy — the Fed can raise interest rates or the government can cut spending/raise taxes to reduce aggregate demand.

questions.question: Country A can produce 100 units of wheat or 50 units of cloth. Country B can produce 60 units of wheat or 60 units of cloth. Country A has comparative advantage in:
questions.explanation: A's opportunity cost of wheat: 0.5 cloth. B's opportunity cost of wheat: 1 cloth. A has the lower opportunity cost for wheat, so A has comparative advantage in wheat.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Tariffs benefit which group in the importing country" is: Domestic consumers.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Domestic producers of the protected good.

questions.question: The Phillips Curve represents the short-run trade-off between:
options: GDP growth and the budget deficit
questions.explanation: The short-run Phillips Curve shows an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment — expansionary policy lowers unemployment but raises inflation.

questions.question: A country that exports more than it imports has a:
questions.explanation: A trade surplus (positive net exports) occurs when the value of exports exceeds the value of imports.

questions.question: According to the Solow model, an increase in the savings rate will:
options: Permanently increase the long-run growth rate
Raise the steady-state level of capital and output per worker
Have no effect on capital accumulation
questions.answer: Raise the steady-state level of capital and output per worker
questions.explanation: A higher savings rate increases investment, raising capital per worker until a new, higher steady state is reached — but the long-run growth rate is unchanged (still driven by tech progress).

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which policy would MOST directly reduce income inequality in a developing country" is: Investing in universal primary education and healthcare.
questions.explanation: True. Investing in universal primary education and healthcare is correct.

questions.question: The concept of 'creative destruction' as described by Schumpeter refers to:
options: New innovations displacing old industries and firms, driving long-run growth
Central bank policy that destroys inflationary expectations
questions.answer: New innovations displacing old industries and firms, driving long-run growth
questions.explanation: Schumpeter argued that capitalism advances through creative destruction — new technologies and firms continuously replace old ones, fueling economic growth.

questions.question: A country running a large current account deficit is most likely:
options: Importing more than it exports and borrowing from abroad
questions.answer: Importing more than it exports and borrowing from abroad
questions.explanation: A current account deficit means a country's imports exceed exports; it must finance the gap by borrowing from or selling assets to foreigners (capital account surplus).

questions.question: In the long-run Phillips Curve framework, attempts to keep unemployment below the natural rate will result in:
options: Accelerating inflation with no long-run reduction in unemployment
questions.answer: Accelerating inflation with no long-run reduction in unemployment
questions.explanation: The long-run Phillips Curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment. Attempts to reduce unemployment below this level lead only to ever-increasing inflation.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following best describes the role of institutions in economic development" is: Institutions are irrelevant; only natural resources determine development.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Strong institutions (rule of law, property rights, anti-corruption) underpin sustained growth.

questions.question: If the Federal Reserve wants to stimulate the economy, it would most likely:
options: Sell government securities on the open market
Lower the federal funds rate target
questions.answer: Lower the federal funds rate target
questions.explanation: Lowering the federal funds rate encourages borrowing and investment, expanding aggregate demand — a standard expansionary monetary policy action.

questions.question: Which of the following is the BEST example of a positive externality?
options: A homeowner maintaining a beautiful garden that neighbors enjoy
A firm dumping waste into a river
questions.answer: A homeowner maintaining a beautiful garden that neighbors enjoy
questions.explanation: The homeowner's private action generates an uncompensated benefit for neighbors — a classic positive externality — meaning the market undersupplies it.

questions.question: In an economy with MPC = 0.8, if the government increases spending by $50 billion, the total increase in GDP (assuming no crowding out) will be:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: $250 billion.
